summary: Excellent audience recording of the legendary 10‑12‑84 Augusta show, highlighted by a soaring Morning Dew and tight Uncle John’s reprise.
review: The tape captures an iconic 1984 performance with Jerry’s blistering riffs on Morning Dew, a primal pre‑drums jam and a memorable Uncle John’s/Playing‑in‑the‑Band reprise that many fans cite as the night’s high point.  Technically it is a very clean audience (AUD) source – warm, balanced and surprisingly detailed for an AUD – though a few listeners note the vocals on Good Lovin' sound forced.  Overall the show is praised as one of the band’s best late‑’80s outings.

ai_rating.rationale: Average user rating ~4.56 ★, with 15/18 reviews praising both performance and sound quality; strong consensus makes the estimate reliable.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Clear, warm and well‑balanced AUD tape; presence of “3D ambience”, good high‑end punch, minimal muddiness. Some note it’s not a true matrix but the audience capture is superior to many Oade recordings.

standout_songs: Playing in the Band / Playing Reprisal
poor_songs: Morning Dew (some reviewers felt vocal delivery was forced)
show_quality.setlist_flow: Fans rave about the seamless transition into Morning Dew, the pre‑drums jam and the uplifting Uncle John’s reprise.  One reviewer suggests moving the disc break after Cold Rain & Snow for smoother flow.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Garcia is repeatedly praised for scorching guitar work on Morning Dew and energetic stage presence; a few notes mention his vocals sounded forced on Good Lovin' but overall his performance is a highlight.
band_member_comments.Phil: Bass work described as solid and “speaker‑blowing”; no major criticisms noted.
band_member_comments.Bob: Bob Weir’s vocal on Good Lovin' called "wacky" by one reviewer, otherwise his rhythm guitar contribution is unremarkable in the comments.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent Mydland’s keyboard reverb is noted as prominent (a true AUD trait) and generally adds depth to the mix; no negative remarks.
band_member_comments.Drums: The pre‑drums jam is called “primal” and the overall drum work receives praise for driving the set’s energy.
